coredata.dc:title: Highly adhesive carbon quantum dots from biogenic amines for prevention of biofilm formation

coredata.dc:description: We demonstrate the synthesis of antibacterial carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through a one-step pyrolysis of biogenic polyamine (PA) and dopamine (DA) mixture. SPM/DA-CQDs synthesized from DA combined with spermine (SPM) exhibit effective antibacterial activity and high adhesion properties on glass and surfaces of polymeric contact lens material. The antimicrobial activity of SPM/DA-CQDs is primarily due to their ability to disrupt the bacterial membrane, and they possess wide spectrum antimicrobial activity against Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Salmonella enterica serovar Enteritidis, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and its multidrug-resistant strain, methicillin-resistant S. aureus). The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of SPM/DA-CQDs against the tested bacteria was >17-fold and >7-fold lower than those of CQDs prepared solely from spermine and dopamine, respectively. The great biocompatibility of our SPM/DA-CQDs is revealed from the in vitro cytotoxicity and hemolysis assays and in vivo evaluation of morphological and physiological changes of rabbit corneas. We further prepared SPM/DA-CQDs-coated coverslips and contact lenses, which display excellent stability and antibiofilm properties. Moreover, in vivo tests show that SPM/DA-CQDs suppress bacterial keratitis (BK) formation in a corneal-injured rabbit model. Our results indicate that SPM/DA-CQDs have great potential as a coating material for inhibition of biofilm formation on contact lenses, and broadly to protect medical devices from contamination.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM images of as-synthesized (a) PUT/DA-CQDs-250, (b) SPD/DA-CQDs-250, and (c) SPM/DA-CQDs-250.

(A) Representative colony formation of S. aureus on LB agar plates in the (a) absence (untreated) and presence (10 μg mL−1) of DA-CQDs-250, SPM/DA-CQDs-250, SPM-CQDs-250, dopamine or spermine. The number of colonies on the plates of untreated, DA-CQDs-250-, SPM/DA-CQDs-250-, SPM-CQDs-250-, dopamine-, and spermine-treated S. aureus were 2042, 402, 0, 501, 612, and 1964 CFU, respectively. (B) Comparison of MICs of DA-CQDs-250, SPM/DA-CQDs-250, SPM-CQDs-250, dopamine, and spermine. Error bars in (B) represent the standard deviation of three repeated measurements.
SEM and TEM images of S. aureus and E. coli cells untreated and after treatment with SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (100 μg mL−1).

Evaluation of antibiofilm activity of SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated HEMA-AAc film. (A) SEM image, (B) crystal violet staining, and (C) colony assay of S. aureus growth on the uncoated HEMA-AAc film and SPM/DA-CQDs-250 coated HEMA-AAc film. An asterisk indicates statistically significant differences (**p < 0.01; n = 3) for the mean value of quantitative data compared to the control group (uncoated HEMA-AAc film).

. Time-course in vivo bacterial keratitis (BK) evaluation of SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated CL (SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL) for the treatment of injured rabbit cornea infected with S. aureus. Treatment of uncoated HEMA-based CL was used as the control (Ctrl) group. (A) Slit-lamp biomicroscopic images, (B) slit-lamp examination scores, and (C) corneal thickness values of rabbit eyes after different treatments. Scale bars in (A): 5 mm. The yellow dash lines in (B) and (C) denote the values for slit-lamp examination and corneal thickness measurements in preoperative eyes, respectively. An asterisk indicates statistically significant differences (*p < 0.05, **p < 0.01; n = 6) for the mean value of quantitative data compared to the Ctrl group at each time point; # p < 0.05 vs. the value at previous time points. Follow-up time point: hour (h); day (d).

. In vivo inhibition of S. aureus-induced bacterial keratitis (BK) by SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL. Treatment of uncoated HEMA-based CL was used as the control (Ctrl) group. (A) Macroscopic views of typescript beneath corneal samples. (B) Photographs of bacterial cultures on mannitol salt agar from the corneal tissues. (C) Quantification of surviving S. aureus in infected corneas. Error bars represent the standard deviation of six repeated measurements. An asterisk indicates statistically significant differences (***p < 0.001; n = 6) as compared with the Ctrl groups. Histological examination of corneal tissue sections by staining with (D) hematoxylin and eosin (H&E) and (E) Gram. Yellow arrows in (D) indicate inflammatory cell infiltration. Ep: epithelium; S: stroma; En: endothelium. Scale bars indicate 100 μm (D) and 50 μm (E).

Schematic representation of (A) one-step synthesis of antibacterial carbon quantum dots (CQDs) from spermine and dopamine, and (B) the CQDs-coated contact lens used to alleviate bacterial keratitis development in the injured cornea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: We demonstrate the synthesis of antibacterial carbon quantum dots (CQDs) through a one-step pyrolysis of biogenic polyamine (PA) and dopamine (DA) mixture. SPM/DA-CQDs synthesized from DA combined with spermine (SPM) exhibit effective antibacterial activity and high adhesion properties on glass and surfaces of polymeric contact lens material. The antimicrobial activity of SPM/DA-CQDs is primarily due to their ability to disrupt the bacterial membrane, and they possess wide spectrum antimicrobial activity against Gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, Salmonella enterica serovar Enteritidis, and Pseudomonas aeruginosa) and Gram-positive bacteria (Staphylococcus aureus and its multidrug-resistant strain, methicillin-resistant S. aureus). The minimal inhibitory concentration (MIC) of SPM/DA-CQDs against the tested bacteria was >17-fold and >7-fold lower than those of CQDs prepared solely from spermine and dopamine, respectively. The great biocompatibility of our SPM/DA-CQDs is revealed from the in vitro cytotoxicity and hemolysis assays and in vivo evaluation of morphological and physiological changes of rabbit corneas. We further prepared SPM/DA-CQDs-coated coverslips and contact lenses, which display excellent stability and antibiofilm properties. Moreover, in vivo tests show that SPM/DA-CQDs suppress bacterial keratitis (BK) formation in a corneal-injured rabbit model. Our results indicate that SPM/DA-CQDs have great potential as a coating material for inhibition of biofilm formation on contact lenses, and broadly to protect medical devices from contamination.

ce:section.ce:para: Bacterial infections in human beings can cause severe health problems and even death. It is well known that many bacteria attach to living or non-living solid surfaces to form structured communities called “biofilms”, embedded within an extracellular polymer matrix [1]. Biofilm formation often allows the aggregation of the bacterial colonies, thereby inducing higher resistance toward antibiotics than for non-attached bacteria [2]. Biofilm-associated infections of indwelling medical devices, such as those used for intravascular access and urinary catheters, are critical issues in the public health field [3]. Furthermore, one of the risk factors for contact lens (CL)-related infection in cornea may be the biofilm adhesion on CL or CL storage cases [4]. About 30% of bacterial isolates from eye infections show the ability to produce biofilms [5]. The major biofilm isolates include Pseudomonas aeruginosa (P. aeruginosa; ~60%), Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus; ~20%), and Staphylococcus epidermidis (~10%) [6]. Bacterial keratitis (BK), a sight-threatening ocular disease is the infection of the cornea caused by bacteria. The symptoms of BK depends on the location of the bacterial infiltration and the virulence of the bacteria and may include acute ocular pain, decreased vision, photophobia, and hyperemia [7]. Furthermore, biofilm formation on CL often results in higher resistance to many antimicrobial agents, such as polyhexamethylene biguanide and polyquaternium used in care solutions [8]. Thus, infectious BK associated with CL can be avoided by eliminating bacterial colonization and contamination of the CL storage case. Consequently, prevention and inhibition of biofilm formation on CL are essential, and can be achieved by antibacterial contact lens solutions.

Recent advances in nanotechnology have led to many promising antibacterial nanomaterials that are effective for biofilm control and treatment [9–11]. Metal-based nanoparticles (NPs), such as copper oxide, silver, titanium dioxide, and zinc oxide NPs (ZnO NPs), possess well-known antibacterial and antibiofilm properties [9–14]. Compared to conventional medical antibiotics, these metal and metal oxide nanomaterials significantly decrease the risk of resistance development via their complex mechanisms of action. Taking the example of ZnO NPs, which reportedly accumulate on bacterial surfaces via electrostatic interactions, and then cause membrane dysfunction by the release of zinc ions, which ultimately result in interruption of the electron transport system [15]. Furthermore, reactive oxygen species (ROS) generation by ZnO NPs also has been suggested as an effective mechanism for interruption of bacterial growth kinetics [16]. However, metallic and metal oxide nanomaterials intended for biomedical applications present some health concerns since they are cytotoxic to most human cells [17]. This has led to the development of non-metal carbon-based nanomaterials that are more biocompatible, and exhibit superior antibacterial activity [18–20].

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are small-sized carbon nanomaterials (usually smaller than 10 nm) with various passivated surfaces, and have optical properties and photocatalytic functions reminiscent of the ones in semiconductor-based quantum dots [21–23]. Many CQDs exhibit good water dispersibility, excellent fluorescent properties, extreme photostability, and low cytotoxicity [23–26]. These superior characteristics of CQDs allow them to be widely employed for sensing and biomedical applications, including fluorescent sensing, cellular imaging, targeted drug delivery, and antibacterial medicines [27–30]. The antimicrobial actions of CQDs arise mainly through ROS generation and/or disruption of the bacterial membrane [31–36]. Furthermore, CQDs functionalized with specific molecules can specifically target bacterial cells, leading to increased antibacterial efficiency [34–37]. However, applications of CQDs for the treatment of biofilms are rare [38,39].

Biogenic polyamines (PAs), such as spermidine (N-(3-aminopropyl)butane-1,4-diamine), spermine (N,N′-bis(3-aminopropyl)butane-1,4-diamine), and their precursor, putrescine (1,4-diaminobutane), are found in virtually all cells of the higher eukaryotes [40]. PAs have both primary and secondary amine groups in the aliphatic hydrocarbon chain and possess positive charge at physiological pH. PAs are essential for various cellular functions, including cell growth, proliferation, cellular reparation, protein synthesis, DNA stabilization, gene transcription and translation, and functioning of ion channels [40,41]. It is known that combining antibiotics (e.g., oxacillin, chloramphenicol, β-lactams) with PAs enhances antibiotic effectiveness against Escherichia coli (E. coli) [42]. In addition, exogenous spermine affects the synthesis of the cell wall in methicillin-resistant S. aureus (MRSA) through its interactions with the penicillin-binding protein 2 (PBP 2) and/or PBP 2-associated multienzyme machineries, thereby enhancing the bactericidal effects of β-lactam antibiotics [43]. Reports have shown that chloramphenicol-conjugated to PAs enhances the antibacterial activity of the antibiotic by using the spermidine-preferential uptake system to increase the internalization of the antibiotic, thereby interfering with protein synthesis via polyamine metabolism, resulting in a decrease in protein and polyamine content in E. coli and S. aureus [44]. Therefore, PAs are candidates with much potential to derive effective antibacterial agents.

In the present study, we have prepared carbon quantum dots (SPM/DA-CQDs) by a one-step heating of a mixture of spermine (SPM) and dopamine [DA; 2-(3,4-dihydroxyphenyl)ethylamine)] (Scheme 1 ). Dopamine has attracted much attention due to its polymeric forms displaying strong adhesion to polymer, glass, and metal surfaces [45]. In comparison with free dopamine and spermine, SPM/DA-CQDs possess superior antibacterial activity against non-multi-drug resistant bacteria, such as E. coli, Salmonella enteritidis (S. enteritidis), P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus, and against the multi-drug resistant (MDR) bacteria, MRSA. Furthermore, SPM/DA-CQDs exhibit strong adhesion to the surface of a glass slide and to contact lenses, enabling its applications as an efficient antibacterial coating. Surfaces coated with SPM/DA-CQDs show effective antibiofilm activity against an S. aureus-formed biofilm. In vitro cytotoxicity and hemolytic assays show that SPM/DA-CQDs have great biocompatibility. We further demonstrate that injured corneas of rabbit eyes treated with an SPM/DA-CQDs-coated contact lens alleviates S. aureus-induced bacterial keratitis (BK). Our results demonstrate that SPM/DA-CQDs are a promising antibiofilm coating agent on various surfaces for biomedical applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of CQDs from dopamine and PAs
ce:section.ce:para: CQDs from dopamine and polyamine (PA/DA-CQDs) were synthesized by a simple heating method. For the synthesis of DA-CQDs, 0.04 g dopamine dissolved in 0.5 mL deionized water was heated in a 20 mL glass sample vial at 230–270 °C for 2 h. For the preparation of PA/DA-CQDs-250, polyamine (0.04 g) and dopamine (0.04 g) mixture dissolved in 0.5 mL deionized water in a 20 mL glass sample vial was heated at 250 °C for 2 h. The solid residue thus obtained was dissolved in 5 mL of deionized water, sonicated for 1 h, and purified by centrifugation with a relative centrifuge force (RCF) of 20000g for 1 h. The supernatant containing DA-CQDs or PA/DA-CQDs-250 was then dialyzed through a dialysis membrane (MWCO = 0.5 kD; Float-A-Lyzer G2, Spectrum Laboratories, Rancho Dominguez, CA, USA) against deionized water (ca. 5 L) for 10 h, changing the water every 2 h. The purified CQDs thus obtained was stored at 4 °C. The CQDs prepared from dopamine, spermidine/dopamine mixture, spermine/dopamine mixture, and putrescine/dopamine mixture at 250 °C are denoted as DA-CQDs-250, SPD/DA-CQDs-250, SPM/DA-CQDs-250, PUT/DA-CQDs-250, respectively. The product yields of CQDs were calculated from the mass of the CQDs after dialysis.

E. coli, S. enteritidis, P. aeruginosa, S. aureus, and MRSA were grown individually in sterile Luria–Bertani (LB) agar plates and a single colony of each bacteria was separated and inoculated in 10 mL of LB media. The inoculum was cultured at 37 °C with continuous shaking (200 rpm) until the absorbance at 600 nm (OD600) reached 1.0 (optical path length: 1.0 cm). The bacterial solution (1.0 mL) was centrifuged at 3000g for 10 min and washed with 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 1.0 mL) twice before use. The minimum inhibitory concentration (MIC) values (>90% of the isolates were inhibited) of the SPM, DA, DA-CQDs-250, SPM-CQDs-250, and SPM/DA-CQDs-250 against five bacterial strains were obtained by the broth microdilution method. First, CQDs were incubated with S. aureus, E. coli, MRSA, P. aeruginosa or S. enteritidis (104 CFU mL−1) in 5 mM sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4) at room temperature for 1 h under shaking (250 rpm). The CQDs-incubated-bacteria samples were cultured in LB media at 37 °C for 18 h with constant shaking at 250 rpm. The solution was serially diluted 107-fold and 100 μL of the diluent was spread on LB agar plate to determine the cell density.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of SPM/DA-CQDs-coated contact lens as an antibacterial surface
ce:section.ce:para: A mixture (1 mL), containing 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA) (790 μL), acrylic acid (AAc) (130 μL), 2,2-diethoxyacetophenone (DEAP, 40 μL), and trimethylolpropane trimethacrylate (TMPTMA, 40 μL), was irradiated under a Blak-Ray 160 W model B-100SP high intensity UV lamp (365 nm; placed 5 cm away) for 30 min for the preparation of HEMA-AAc film [46]. The copolymer membranes thus obtained were immersed in phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; pH 7.4, containing 137 mM NaCl, 2.7 mM KCl, 10 mM Na2HPO4, and 2.0 mM KH2PO4) overnight to remove unformed polymer, initiator, and cross-linker. Then, the sample was dried in vacuum for 48 h. The copolymer membrane (HEMA-AAc film) was cut into a circular-shape membrane (diameter ~0.8 cm). The HEMA-AAc film was placed in 12-well microplates and soaked with SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (200 μg mL−1) for 30 min. The SPM/DA-CQDs-250 adsorbed on the HEMA-AAc film were quantified by the fluorescence intensity of the soaking solution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vitro antibiofilm activity of SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL
ce:section.ce:para: SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL was incubated with S. aureus (106 CFU mL−1) in a 12-well plate at 37 ℃ with mild shaking (50 rpm) for 48 h, and washed thrice with PBS. The formed biofilm samples were fixed by 4% paraformaldehyde, then stained with 0.5% crystal violet for 15 min, solubilized with 500 μL of 30% acetic acid, and their absorbances at 595 nm (OD595) were measured. The formed biofilm samples were also transferred to a 15 mL conical tube containing 2 mL PBS, then vigorously vortexed for 9 min. The suspension was collected and diluted 10-fold by PBS, then spotted on LB agar plates. After incubation at 37 °C for 24 h, the colonies were counted. The biofilm formed was also observed by scanning electron microscope (SEM).

All the animal experiments were carried out as per the ARVO (Association for Research in Vision and Ophthalmology) Statement for the Use of Animals in Ophthalmic and Vision Research and were approved by the Institutional Review Board (Chang Gung University). Adult New Zealand white rabbits (6 nos) of 16–20 weeks age and 3.0–3.5 kg weight from National Laboratory Animal Breeding and Research Center (Taipei, Taiwan) were used for the therapeutic efficacy evaluation. The induced bacterial keratitis (BK) model was performed with a little modification of a previous report [47]. The rabbits were anesthetized intramuscularly with a 1:1 mixture of tiletamine hydrochloride and zolazepam hydrochloride (Zoletil; Virbac, Carros, France) at a dose of 2.5 mg kg−1 body weight and xylazine hydrochloride (Rompun; Bayer, Leverkusen, Germany) with a dose of 1 mg kg−1 body weight. Two drops of topical ocular anesthetic, 0.5% proparacaine hydrochloride ophthalmic solution (Alcaine; Alcon-Couvreur, Puurs, Belgium) was also given. A 3 mm central corneal scratch on one eye was induced using a scalpel, then 20 μL of S. aureus solution (2 × 107 CFU) was dropped on the eye. The eyes were held closed for 2 min, and SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL was placed on the eye for 4 h. Ophthalmic evaluations were performed before surgery by examining the bilateral eyes of the 6 rabbits and also at 12 h post-operation, and once daily for the next 3 days. Slit-lamp biomicroscopy was employed to study the morphology of the anterior segment of the eye, including iris, corneal and lens clarity and the degree of anterior chamber activity and the evaluations were made using the ocular grading method [48]. The postoperative inflammation was evaluated and graded 0–4 by recording the seven different parameters such as iritis, conjunctival injection, chemosis, fibrin accumulation, hypopyon, stromal edema, and stromal infiltrate. The biomicroscopic examination score of individual parameters was summed up to give a total score ranging from 0 for the normal eye to 28 for the maximally inflamed eye, for each group. Ultrasonic pachymeter was used to determine the central corneal thickness and the result of 10 readings were averaged. After the experiment, the animals were euthanized with CO2 gas. A mannitol salt agar (MSA) colony assay was used for determining the number of viable bacteria in the tissues. After homogenization of the excised corneas (~0.2 g) in 2.0 mL of sterile PBS, the samples were diluted 20-fold and 0.1 mL of the diluted samples were plated onto MSA plates and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h. For histological analysis, after fixing the corneal tissue in 4% paraformaldehyde, they were cut into sections of 5-μm thickness and stained with H&E and Gram staining.

See the Supporting Information for the details on the materials, characterization of CQDs, SEM and transmission electron microscope (TEM) image of CQDs-treated bacteria, bacterial viability of S. aureus treated with SPM/DA-CQDs, evaluation of bacterial membrane permeability, preparation of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 coated-glass slide for antibiofilm assay, and in vitro biocompatibility evaluation.

First, the CQDs were synthesized by direct pyrolysis of dopamine (DA) solution (80 mg mL−1, 0.5 mL) in a muffle furnace at 230 to 270 °C for 2 h. The CQDs samples thus obtained were dispersed in deionized water, sonicated, and then centrifuged to remove larger particles. This was followed by purification by dialysis against deionized water, and then the stock solutions were stored at 4 °C. The CQDs synthesized from dopamine at 230, 250, and 270 °C are denoted as DA-CQDs-230, DA-CQDs-250, and DA-CQDs-270, respectively. Among the DA-CQDs synthesized at temperatures from 230 °C to 270 °C, DA-CQDs-250 had the highest yield (11.4%), and the maximum positive zeta potential (ζ) of about +31 mV when prepared in sodium phosphate buffer (pH 7.4, 5 mM) (Table S1). The DA-CQDs are probably formed through dehydration, polymerization between phenol and amine groups, carbonization, and surface passivation [49]. During the carbonization step, the activated polymeric intermediates are carbonized to nitrogen-doped polycyclic aromatic rings, which are further condensed to form the multilayered core, whereas noncarbonized dopamine polymers self-assemble on the carbon core leading to surface passivation [50–52]. The heating of dopamine at 270 °C resulted in the formation of poorly soluble black carbon due to extreme pyrolysis and carbonization (Fig. S1C). As a result, the yield of the obtained CQDs was <1%.

TEM images of DA-CQDs synthesized at three different temperatures indicate a particle size of <10 nm (Fig. S2). The UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectra of the DA-CQDs are presented in Fig. S3A(a) and S3A(b), respectively. The DA-CQDs exhibit peaks at 275 nm corresponding to the π → π* transitions, suggesting the formation of a graphite carbon structure in their cores [53,54]. The shoulder peaks observed above 300 nm are attributed to the n → π* transitions of the CO and CN bonds [50–52]. The doping of the core and the surface with heteroatoms highly affect π-electron conjugation of the carbon core and the n → π* transitions of the surface functional groups, and therefore the fluorescence emission of CQDs [53–57]. Compared to DA-CQDs-230 and DA-CQDs-270, the DA-CQDs-250 exhibits a relatively strong and broad fluorescence band at 475 nm with an excitation wavelength of 365 nm, suggesting the formation of a highly heterogenous conjugated carbon core and/or multiple surface emissive traps [53–57]. With quinine as a reference (fluorescence quantum yield (QY) = 54% in 0.1 M H2SO4), the QY of the DA-CQDs-250 were determined to be 4.3%, which is much higher than of DA-CQDs-230 and DA-CQDs-270 (QY < 0.1%).

We examined the antibacterial activity of unheated dopamine (5 μg mL−1) and DA-CQDs (5 μg mL−1) against E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. S4A). Free dopamine exhibits negligible antibacterial activity toward the representative Gram-positive (S. aureus) and Gram-negative (E. coli) bacteria in comparison with the untreated control. In contrast, the DA-CQDs-250 exhibit the best antibacterial activity among the prepared DA-CQDs, probably due to their higher positive surface charge (ζ ≈ +31 mV; Table S1). The highly positively charged DA-CQDs-250 interact with the bacterial membrane through strong electrostatic interaction, affecting its integrity, leading to the leakage of cytoplasmic organelles and thereby the imbalance of cytoplasmic osmotic pressure [58]. The dopamine acts not only as the carbon source for the formation of the carbon core but also as functional molecules or dopamine-derived moieties on the surface of the as-formed DA-CQDs during pyrolysis. The polymeric dopamine anchored on the surfaces of the DA-CQDs confers specific antibacterial properties. The polymeric dopamine on the CQDs may also contribute to the attachment of the DA-CQDs to the bacterial cell membrane, which causes its disintegration [59]. Dopamine undergoes oxidation, rearrangement, and polymerization to form polydopamine (PDA). Thus, the various functional groups (amino, imino, hydroxyl and catechol groups) in PDA make it strongly bind to various solid surfaces in a hydrated environment. Furthermore, PDA coated on E. coli restricts bacterial cell division and reduces the permeability of nutrients and metabolic wastes, leading to cell death [59]. This bacterial membrane adhesion ability of PDA also enhances the interaction between PDA-coated nanomaterials and bacteria [60]. Accordingly, polymeric dopamine on nanoparticles can provide a potent mode of interaction with bacteria that boosts antimicrobial activity.

Biogenic polyamines (PAs) are biocompatible, and possess many amine groups that give multiple positive charges to the molecules [40,41]. Our previous studies revealed that the pyrolysis products obtained from PAs exhibit great antibacterial activity due to their highly positive charge as a result of some non-pyrolyzed PA molecules on the CQDs surface [61–63]. The PA moieties on CQDs enable their strong interaction with phospholipids, peptidoglycans, and porins, which in turn disrupts the bacterial membrane. To improve the antibacterial activity of CQDs, we mixed dopamine with biogenic polyamines, namely, putrescine (PUT), spermidine (SPD) or spermine (SPM), and then heated the mixture at 250 °C for the synthesis of PA/DA-CQDs-250. The yields of all three types of PA/DA-CQDs-250 are higher than 13% (Table S1). Although the UV–vis absorption and fluorescence spectral profiles of the PA/DA-CQDs-250 (Fig. S3B) resemble that of DA-CQDs-250 (Fig. S3A), the particle sizes of the PA/DA-CQDs-250 are much smaller than those of DA-CQDs-250 (>5 nm). The as-synthesized PUT/DA-CQDs-250, SPD/DA-CQDs-250, and SPM/DA-CQDs-250 appear to be monodisperse, with average diameters of 1.9 ± 0.2 nm, 3.6 ± 0.4, and 4.2 ± 0.5 nm, respectively (each was determined from 100 counts) as evident from the TEM images (Fig. 1 ). The polyamine-mediated condensation of dopamine leads to a higher degree of carbonization, resulting in the formation of smaller particles [64–66]. The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra and X-ray photoelectron spectra (XPS) support our reasoning (Fig. S5). The FT-IR absorption spectrum of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 shows peaks at 726, 1371, 1477, and 1640 cm−1 corresponding to imide CNC out-of-plane bending, imide CNC axial stretching, a semiquinone carbonyl vibration, and CN stretching, respectively [67]. In the N1s XPS spectrum, the deconvoluted imide CNHC (399.4 eV) peak suggests that thermally-driven condensation of dopamine with spermine forms an imide via a Michael-type addition [67]. Moreover, diverse functional groups are observed in the FT-IR spectrum that clearly demonstrates that the PA/DA-CQDs retain many of the chemical structures of their precursors even after heating at 250 °C for 2 h. Therefore, we suggest that the pyrolysis of dopamine and the polyamines at 250 °C preserves the properties of the precursor molecules on the CQDs, which provide functional groups and a highly positive surface charge.

Next, we compared the antibacterial activity of PUT/DA-CQDs-250, SPD/DA-CQDs-250, and SPM/DA-CQDs-250 against E. coli and S. aureus (Fig. S4B). SPM/DA-CQDs-250 shows superior synergistic antibacterial activity toward the tested bacteria when compared to DA-CQDs-250, PUT/DA-CQDs-250, and SPD/DA-CQDs-250. We attribute the higher antibacterial activity of the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 to their higher positive surface charge (ζ ≈ +33 mV; Table S1) as a result of some non-pyrolyzed spermine-like molecules/moieties anchored on the surface of the CQDs. The MICvalues of the DA-CQDs-250, SPM/DA-CQDs-250, and SPM-CQDs-250 were determined by a standard dilution method in different non-MDR bacterial strains (E. coli, S. enteritidis, P. aeruginosa, and S. aureus) and in MRSA. The typical colony formation assays of S. aureus treated with various types of CQDs (10 μg mL−1) are shown in Fig. 2 A. Consistent with the above result, SPM/DA-CQDs-250 possess the highest antibacterial activity against the tested strains of bacteria. The MIC values of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 for the tested bacteria were in the range of 2–8 μg mL−1 (Fig. 2B). The MIC value of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 was approximately 7-fold, 17-fold, 150-fold, and 600-fold lower than those of DA-CQDs-250, SPM-CQDs-250, free dopamine, and free spermine, respectively. Compared to free dopamine and spermine, the high density of spermine, dopamine and/or their pyrolytic products on the surface of CQDs endow them with properties that permit to interact with the bacterial membrane and consequently have much lower MIC values. We further noted that the MIC value of our SPM/DA-CQDs-250 is lower than most reported antibacterial nanomaterials [9–16,18–20], including carbon-based nanomaterials such as carbon dots, graphene oxides, carbon nanotubes, and fullerenes [18–20,30–39,68].

TEM and SEM images in Fig. 3 show serious disruption of S. aureus and E. coli cell membranes, for those the bacteria treated with SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (100 μg mL−1) for 2 h, while those of the untreated group are smooth and intact. The small size of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (4.2 nm) and highly abundant pyrolytic dopamine and spermine molecules/moieties on their surfaces enable multivalent interaction with the bacterial membrane. Thereby these CQDs synergistically destabilize the cell membranes and inhibit the membrane synthesis. It has been demonstrated that spermine can adsorb onto the bacterial membrane due to its polycationic characteristics [69]. Bacteria primarily synthesize only two polyamines, putrescine, and spermidine, with their intracellular concentrations within the range of 1.0 to 30.0 mM [70]. In contrast, spermine is distributed in many animal tissues but is not synthesized by bacteria [71]. Moreover, the growth-inhibiting properties of spermine have been reported against various strains of bacteria [72]. Studies indicate that spermine can increase the antibiotic susceptibility in gram-negative bacteria as well as in gram-positive bacteria (including MRSA), due to its interaction with membrane proteins such as porin and polyamine-transporting proteins [42]. Therefore, the positive charge of the spermine residues on the CQDs provides selectivity, and favors interactions with negatively charged bacterial membranes and specific proteins. On the other hand, the hydrophobic features of the dopamine domains on the CQDs enable interaction with fatty acyl chains and cause segregation of membrane lipids [73]. These interactions compromise the integrity of the bacterial membranes, and thus, provoke the cytosol to leak out of the bacterial cell, thereby leading to cell death. The SPM/DA-CQDs-250 readily bind to the E. coli and S. aureus, and cause aggregation of the bacteria in just one min of incubation, thereby supporting our hypothesis of rapid and strong interaction between the CQDs and bacteria (Fig. S6).

We further performed SYTO 9/propidium iodide (PI) staining to study the effect of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 on bacterial membrane integrity (Fig. S7) and determined the release of β-galactosidase from membrane-defective bacteria (Fig. S8). The time-course fluorescence microscopy images of S. aureus treated with SPM/DA-CQDs-250 exhibit a gradual decrease of green fluorescence of the SYTO 9 and an increase in the red fluorescence of PI, revealing the decreasing bacterial viability and disruption of the bacterial cell membranes (Fig. S7). Furthermore, the cytoplasmic β-galactosidase released from SPM/DA-CQDs-250-treated E. coli was significantly higher than from the control, spermine, and dopamine groups, further supporting that the CQDs induce significant disruption of the bacterial cell membrane (Fig. S8). Although some nanomaterials derive their antibacterial ability mainly through the generation of ROS [32,34,39,74,75], 2′,7′-dichlorodihydrofluorescein diacetate (DCFH-DA) assay excludes the possibility of ROS-induced inhibition of bacterial cells by SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (Fig. S9). All of the above results indicate that the positively charged SPM/DA-CQDs-250 exhibit superior antibacterial activity through interaction with and subsequent rupturing of the bacterial cell membrane, leading to the leakage of cellular components, and eventually cell death without ROS generation.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and evaluation of antibiofilm activity of CQDs-coated glass
ce:section.ce:para: To make an antibiofilm surface, an SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated glass coverslip was prepared by one-step pyrolysis of a mixture of dopamine and spermine on the glass (diameter: 0.8 cm) at 250 °C. We next immersed the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated glass in DI water, followed by sonication (200 W) for 1 h. The SPM/DA-CQDs-250 remained on the glass surface, indicating that the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 exhibit great surface adhesion on glass, which may be attributed to the superior adhesive characteristic of polydopamine on the CQDs (Fig. S10). The cross-sectional SEM image shows a uniform film of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 with a thickness of ~0.28 μm (Fig. S10B(a)). Top-view SEM image shows the high surface roughness of the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 layer (Fig. S10B(b)). The transmittance of the coated glass in visible light region (400–700 nm) is higher than 80%. The SPM-CQDs-250-coated glass, made by the sole pyrolysis of spermine at the same temperature (250 °C) on the glass, is prone to detachment during sonication (data not shown). Also, we tried direct coating of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 on the glass by simple immersion of the glass substrate into the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 solution. However, the adsorbed SPM/DA-CQDs-250 also tend to detach from the glass surface during sonication (data not shown). These results reveal that the stable CQDs-coated glass could only be obtained by in situ formation through heat-driven pyrolysis.

We evaluated the antibiofilm activity of the CQDs-coated glass by crystal violet staining, SYTO 9/PI staining, and SEM imaging (Fig. S11). Qualitative results, such as SYTO 9/PI staining and SEM imaging, reveal that the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-based surface exhibits great antibiofilm ability (Fig. S11A and B). In addition, we used a classic dye, crystal violet, to stain the components of the biofilm, including bacterial cells via interaction with their glycoproteins [76]. SPM/DA-CQDs-250 inhibit ~85% of biofilm formation on the glass in comparison with non-coated glass (Fig. S11C). The cell death caused by the contact-active antibacterial coatings is mainly due to the damage of the cellular membrane [77]. Indeed, the SEM image of S. aureus in contact with the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated glass shows that the spherical morphology of the bacteria was disrupted (Fig. S11B(b)).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and evaluation of antibiofilm activity of CQDs-adsorbed contact lens
ce:section.ce:para: To further prepare an SPM/DA-CQDs-250-adsorbed contact lens (CL), we used a 2-hydroxyethyl methacrylate (HEMA)-based polymer film (diameter: 0.8 cm; thickness: 0.2 mm), which is the main component of contact lenses. We soaked the HEMA film in SPM/DA-CQDs-250 suspension (0.5 mL; 0.2 mg mL−1 in deionized water). Around 65.3% of the SPM/DA-CQDs were adsorbed on the HEMA copolymerized with an acrylic acid (AAc) film (HEMA-AAc). After incubation for 30 min, the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-adsorbed copolymer film was washed with DI water and sonicated. We observed no obvious leakage of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 from the film sample as evident from undetectable fluorescence of the sonicated solution. We then evaluated the antibiofilm activity of the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-adsorbed HEMA-AAc film by SEM imaging, crystal violet staining, and viable plate counting (Fig. 4 ). SEM shows that the bacterial count significantly decreased in comparison with the control (uncoated copolymer film), indicating the excellent antibiofilm activity of the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated HEMA-AAc film (Fig. 4A). The results of crystal violet assay demonstrate a decrease of >80% biofilm formation on the CL coated with SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (Fig. 4B). Similarly, the viable bacterial counts reveal that the SPM/DA-CQDs coating material inhibits ~82% of biofilm formation on the copolymer film (Fig. 4C). These results strongly suggest that the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 has great potential to prevent the formation of biofilm on the surface of CL.

After the demonstration of the superior antibacterial activity of SPM/DA-CQDs-250, we subsequently evaluated another important property, their biocompatibility for in vivo applications. We evaluated biocompatibility by cytotoxicity and hemolysis assays using SIRC (Statens Seruminstitut Rabbit Cornea) cells and human red blood cells (RBCs), respectively. SIRC cells, i.e., the rabbit corneal epithelial cells, were treated with SPM/DA-CQDs-250 in the following study. Under microscopy observation, we did not observe significant morphological changes for the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-treated cells, even with concentrations of up to 100 μg mL−1 (Fig. S12A). The cytotoxicity of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 towards SIRC cells in MEM medium containing 10% FBS was further assessed using an MTT assay (Fig. S12B). The cell viability of the CQDs-treated SIRC cells with concentrations as high as 100 μg mL−1 (>10-fold MIC for tested bacteria) do not show a significant decrease compared to their untreated counterparts (p > 0.05; n = 5). In order to mimic the corneal environment, the cytotoxicity of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 towards SIRC cells was also evaluated in a serum-free medium. Again, SPM/DA-CQDs-250 showed negligible cytotoxicity toward SIRC cells versus untreated cells (p > 0.05; n = 5) (Fig. S13). Calcein-AM/EthD-1 cell viability staining results also demonstrate the low cytotoxicity of the SPM/DA-CQDs-250, since almost all of the cells exhibit green fluorescence (Fig. S12C). Compared to bacterial cell membranes, the presence of more cholesterol in the plasma membranes of mammalian cells, strengthens membrane integrity and lowers the membrane potential, resulting in the higher tolerance of mammalian cells towards the SPM/DA-CQDs-250.

Next, to understand whether SPM/DA-CQDs-250 released from the HEMA-based CL causes toxicity, we examined the viability of SIRC cells in a medium soaked with SPM/DA-CQDs-25-coated HEMA CL. First, we soaked the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated CL in the culture medium for 24 h. Then, we removed the CL and used the medium to culture SIRC cells for 24 h. The medium solution did not cause a significant decrease in the viability of SIRC cells (p > 0.05; n = 5), indicative that very few SPM/DA-CQDs-250 are released from CL (data not shown). In addition, we evaluated the biocompatibility of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 by a hemolysis assay using human RBCs treated with the CQDs at various concentrations (10–200 μg mL−1, Fig. S14). No significant hemolytic reaction was observed, even with up to 200 μg mL−1, which is > 25-fold higher than the MIC of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 toward bacteria. Therefore, the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 exhibit significant toxicity toward bacterial cells, negligible cytotoxicity towards mammalian cells, as well as no hemolytic effect to RBCs, which are excellent properties required for an efficient antibacterial material. ROS and comet assays also were conducted for investigating the biosafety of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 (Fig. S15). Our results revealed the CQDs did not cause significantly genotoxic and oxidative damage even at the high concentration (200 μg mL−1). Further, in vivo biocompatibility of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 were evaluated in rabbit eyes (Fig. S16). The SPM/DA-CQDs-250 solution (200 μg mL−1; 50 μL) was dropped on the rabbit eye surface. After 14 consecutive days, the cornea in SPM/DA-CQDs-250 treated groups were almost the same as that of the control group (receiving PBS without nanomaterial), the anterior chamber was quiet (Fig. S16A). The corneal thickness and corneal endothelial cell morphology and density of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 treated group also were similar to that of the control groups (Fig. S16B−D). These results indicate the potential of SPM/DA-CQDs-250 as a highly compatible antibiofilm agent. Dopamine has been used for antibiofilm surface applications in combination with metallic nanomaterials and antibacterial enzymes [78–80]; however, these materials have some issues that need to be resolved. For example, metallic nanomaterials exhibit potent cytotoxicity, while antibacterial enzymes may need to overcome the risk of bacterial resistance. In contrast, SPM/DA-CQDs-250 possess high biocompatibility and can combat antibiotic-resistant bacteria through strong multivalent interactions with bacterial membranes, thereby showing their great potential for antibiofilm applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibiofilm activity of SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated contact lens in vivo
ce:section.ce:para: To evaluate the antibiofilm efficiency of the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated HEMA-based CL (SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL) in vivo, we used a modified experimental bacterial keratitis (BK) model [47]. S. aureus solution was first applied to infect the scratched cornea of rabbit eyes, and then uncoated HEMA-based CL [control (Ctrl) group] or SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL was placed on the injured corneal tissues for 4 h. The changes in corneal thickness and anterior segment morphometry of the eyes of the rabbits that wore the CL were recorded (Fig. 5 ). In normal eyes, the corneas were clear with a mean corneal thickness value of ca. 385 μm (yellow dash line in Fig. 5C), and the anterior chambers were quiet (Fig. 5A). Also, the slit-lamp examination (SLE) scores before bacterial infection were relatively low (<2) (Fig. 5B). At the follow-up after 12 h, the infected rabbit eyes from the Ctrl groups showed a significantly higher slit-lamp examination score (10 ± 1.4), and a significantly thicker corneal layer (466.8 ± 30.7 μm) (p < 0.05). Corneal stromal infiltration and haze were observed by slit-lamp biomicroscopy (Fig. 5A), indicating that several components, including teichoic acid, α-toxin, γ-toxin, Panton-Valentine leukocidin, superantigen-like protein, and elastase secreted from the bacteria, which may contribute to the underlying pathological corneal inflammation and tissue damage [81]. In contrast, for the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL groups, lower SLE score (6.7 ± 0.2), and thinner corneal layer (384 ± 20.2 μm) were observed. It is interesting that after 3 days of bacterial infection, the SLE score in the Ctrl groups increased to 12.5 and the thickness level of these injured corneas increased to 731.4 μm. However, in the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL groups, the SLE score decreased to 2.1, and no significant change in corneal thickness could be detected. These results suggest that biofilm formation on the non-coated CL, leading to the release of S. aureus from the biofilm and further bacterial entry to cornea via the tissue wound, eventually culminates in the interaction with the corneal stroma and the development of severe BK symptoms. Hence, we can conclude that, in comparison with the Ctrl groups, the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL exhibits greater antibiofilm efficacy by demonstrating successful inhibition of biofilm formation on the CL.

At the follow-up after 3 days, tissue samples were collected for further analysis. The photographs of the corneal specimens in Fig. 6 A show that the tissue in the Ctrl group is cloudy, whereas in the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL group it is relatively clear. Poor tissue clarity could be attributed to bacterial infection-induced corneal inflammation and tissue matrix remodeling, which consequently result in the formation of disorganized and opaque extracellular matrices [82]. The S. aureus in the corneal tissues was quantified by a Mannitol salt agar (MSA) colony (Fig. 6B and C). The bacterial count in the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL group was significantly lower than its Ctrl tissue counterpart (p < 0.001), which further verifies the excellent antibiofilm performance of our biogenic amine-based CQDs. Fig. 6D and E demonstrate the corneal histological findings for the various groups. The results of H&E staining indicate that obvious corneal edema accompanied by many infiltrated inflammatory cells (indicated by yellow arrows) in the stromal tissue layer of the Ctrl group (Fig. 6D). In addition, the numerous dark purple clusters of S. aureus were observed in the Ctrl group with severe BK symptoms, by Gram staining, but not in the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL group that wore the biogenic amine-based CQDs-coated HEMA-based CL (Fig. 6E), supporting the high antibacterial activity of the CQDs.

Simple pyrolysis of a mixture of spermine and dopamine allows one to synthesize broad-spectrum antimicrobial CQDs, including against MDR bacteria. Compared to the CQDs synthesized solely from dopamine, spermine, or other tested polyamines, SPM/DA-CQDs-250 exhibit superior antibacterial activity as a result of their high positive charge and specific functional groups on their surfaces, leading to bacterial agglutination and disruption of membrane integrity. Cytotoxicity and hemolysis assays further demonstrate that the SPM/DA-CQDs-250 exhibit high biocompatibility. The SPM/DA-CQDs-250 preserves the functionality of spermine and dopamine on their surfaces, which confer the CQDs coated on HEMA-based film with high stability. Moreover, the SPM/DA-CQDs-250-coated HEMA-AAc film possesses excellent activity for inhibition of biofilm formation. We further demonstrate that the SPM/DA-CQDs-250/CL can prevent bacterial keratitis development via a corneal injured rabbit model. The SPM/DA-CQDs could become a potential alternative to conventional contact lens solutions for the prevention of microbial contamination of contact lenses, as well as to treat the infection caused bacterial keratitis.
